concepts.name: Hydraulic Excavation as Disruption
concepts.definition: Hydraulic technology initially underperformed cable-actuated excavators in large excavation but offered compactness, simplicity, and suitability for small jobs.

concepts.name: Sustaining vs. Disruptive Trajectories
concepts.definition: Cable incumbents improved buckets, reach, and heavy-duty performance for existing customers while hydraulic entrants improved from small jobs toward mainstream excavation.

concepts.name: Application Foothold
concepts.definition: The first market where a disruptive technology's different attributes matter enough despite inferior mainstream performance.

arguments.claim: Incumbents were not blind to hydraulics; their customers did not initially need the hydraulic value proposition.
arguments.evidence: Cable shovel makers pioneered or experimented with hydraulics, but heavy excavation customers valued capacity and durability more than compactness.
The excavator case shows the same mechanism as HDDs in a different industry: incumbents win sustaining battles but entrants win when a new technology starts in a non-mainstream application.

cases.name: Cable-actuated vs hydraulic excavators
cases.context: Large contractors used cable machines; smaller contractors and different jobs could benefit from hydraulic compactness and flexibility.
cases.illustrates: A technology can be inferior for one job and superior for another.
cases.lesson: Segment markets by job and performance dimension, not by industry label.

logicChains.description: Hydraulics underperform in heavy digging → cable incumbents' customers reject them → entrants serve small-bucket applications → hydraulic performance improves → entrants move into mainstream excavation.

terminology.definition: Traditional excavator architecture optimized for heavy excavation with cables and mechanical systems.
Newer architecture using hydraulic systems, initially stronger in compactness and control than heavy-duty performance.
Initial use context where different performance attributes matter.

nuance: Incumbents may invent or understand the technology yet still fail to commercialize it in the right market.
The case prevents overfitting the theory to the fast-moving HDD industry.
